questions.question: Explain the difference between shared memory and message passing IPC mechanisms. Mention which one is faster and why.
keyPoints: Shared memory allows direct access to common memory region
Message passing requires data to be copied through kernel
Shared memory is faster due to zero-copy overhead
Message passing provides easier synchronization
Shared memory requires explicit synchronization primitives

questions.question: What is a Semaphore? Differentiate between binary and counting semaphores with an example.
keyPoints: Semaphore is a synchronization primitive
Binary semaphore has values 0 and 1 only
Counting semaphore can have non-negative integer values
Used for resource allocation and process synchronization
Example: Binary for mutual exclusion, counting for multiple instances

questions.question: Define Critical Section and list the three requirements that a solution to the critical section problem must satisfy.
keyPoints: Critical Section is code accessing shared resources
Requirement 1: Mutual Exclusion - only one process in CS at a time
Requirement 2: Progress - no process waits unnecessarily
Requirement 3: Bounded Waiting - limited number of waits

questions.question: What is the Producer-Consumer problem? Explain how bounded buffer solves this problem with synchronization.
keyPoints: Producer creates items, consumer consumes them
Bounded buffer has finite capacity
When buffer full, producer blocks
When buffer empty, consumer blocks
Uses semaphores for empty and full slots

questions.question: Explain Named Pipe (FIFO) and state two advantages over unnamed pipes.
keyPoints: Named Pipe (FIFO) is a special file in file system
Exists until explicitly deleted
Unrelated processes can communicate using FIFO name
Advantages: persistence, unrelated process communication
Bidirectional or unidirectional communication possible

questions.question: What is a Race Condition? How does synchronization prevent race conditions?
keyPoints: Race condition occurs when outcome depends on execution timing
Happens with concurrent access to shared data
Synchronization ensures mutual exclusion
Mechanisms: semaphores, mutexes, monitors
Only one process accesses critical section at a time

questions.question: List and briefly explain four different IPC mechanisms available in Unix/Linux systems.
keyPoints: Pipe: unidirectional communication channel between related processes
Message Queue: kernel-managed message list with types
Shared Memory: direct access to common memory region
Signal: asynchronous notification to processes
Socket: endpoint for network/local communication

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the Producer-Consumer problem using bounded buffer. Also write pseudocode for both producer and consumer processes using semaphores.
keyPoints: Bounded buffer with N slots
Two semaphores: empty (N) and full (0)
One mutex for mutual exclusion
Producer: wait(empty), wait(mutex), add item, signal(mutex), signal(full)
Consumer: wait(full), wait(mutex), remove item, signal(mutex), signal(empty)
Prevents race condition and deadlocks

questions.question: Explain the critical section problem and discuss Peterson's solution for two processes. Also explain why Peterson's solution works.
keyPoints: Critical section: code accessing shared resources
Three requirements: mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting
Peterson's solution uses two variables: flag[] and turn
Process sets flag[i]=true and turn=j before entering
Waits while flag[j] && turn==j
Satisfies all three requirements for two processes

questions.question: Discuss the different signal handling mechanisms in operating systems. Explain how signals are generated, delivered, and handled with an example.
keyPoints: Signals are software interrupts for asynchronous events
Generation: hardware, software, or user action
Delivery: kernel sends signal to target process
Handling: default action, ignore, or custom handler
Example: SIGINT (Ctrl+C) terminates process by default
signal() system call registers handler
Signal handlers are async-safe functions

questions.question: Compare Pipes, Message Queues, Shared Memory, and Sockets as IPC mechanisms. Discuss their advantages, disadvantages, and suitable use cases.
keyPoints: Pipe: simple, unidirectional, related processes only
Message Queue: asynchronous, structured messages, kernel-managed
Shared Memory: fastest, requires explicit synchronization
Sockets: network capable, client-server model
Pipe: good for parent-child communication
Message Queue: good for client-server with message types
Shared Memory: good for high-speed data exchange
Socket: good for network and distributed systems
